title: Verify signatures from a list of public keys
body:
Add the ability to use an on-disk trust store to verify signatures. Also allow the user to trust any known fingerprint instead of having to specify one.

The reason here is that we now have a number of different keys we have used to sign our images over time as they have been rotated. Being able to supply someone with a keystore they can use to verify any of our images with is valuable to us.

comments.body:
> That’s worries me quite a bit. standalone-verify is a low-level debugging tool, inherently vulnerable to TOCTOU. I think in any kind of production use, almost all users should use policy.json set up at the point of consumption (e.g. for CRI-O or podman pull).

I agree completely that verification at time of use is preferable, unfortunately, I don't feel the signing landscape is there yet. I'm looking forward to registry references making it better. Verifying at point of use is not too bad when pulling from the origin registry, but is hard for users who have mirrored the image and have changed the location, which is part of the signature.

There is a TOCTOU problem when using tags, but we're using digests. If you verify a digest is signed then it's safe to use that digest anywhere, at any time, because an image referred to by digest is immutable.

At the moment, the only good solution we have to help people verify images on a registry using the signature extension mechanism seems to be standalone verify. This pr makes that slightly easier, and some customers have asked us how to verify in place.

This is by no means the way I want customers doing this long term, but I don't have a better option for them right now. I'm looking at ways to help them verify representations on disk as part of a mirror operation, or to enable verification on pull from a mirror registry. Any thoughts welcome!

> is hard for users who have mirrored the image and have changed the location, which is part of the signature.

Is `remapIdentity` in `policy.json` conceptually not sufficient, or somehow broken for this purpose? Sure, that requires some kind of integration between the software that configures mirrors to access and `policy.json`. Is that the hurdle? Or something else?

> I'm looking at ways to help them verify representations on disk as part of a mirror operation, or to enable verification on pull from a mirror registry.

I’d expect the software that _does_ the mirroring to verify signatures when reading from the upstream source (confirming that it is mirroring the right thing), and should be possible using `policy.json`. Do you mean that you are looking at some kind of after-mirroring sanity check of the data? Or is `policy.json` just not applicable because the software that does the mirroring is a different implementation?

---

> There is a TOCTOU problem when using tags, but we're using digests. If you verify a digest is signed then it's safe to use that digest anywhere, at any time, because an image referred to by digest is immutable.

That’s a good point. Fair enough, I’d still recommend looking for ways to to use `policy.json` instead, but if you continue to think this is the only practical way, let’s do it.

It's not the only way, it's low hanging fruit.

I didn't know policy.json could remap. I'll look into how we could get customers using it. At the moment the mirroring tool we use doesn't copy signatures using the extension to the registry, so I have a long hill to climb to get people doing things right!

(BTW if you happened to be revisiting the larger mirroring design now, consider using sigstore-formatted signatures instead. It means a new set of keys, a new signature format, and a new signature transport mechanism, but the signatures are stored in ordinary registries, without the huge hassle of separate lookaside servers, or being restricted to registry implementations with x-registry-supports-signatures — and the signatures are subject to ordinary registry access controls.

If you already have a “simple signing” GPG workflow that works, I don’t think there’s any reason to move; but if you were building this entirely from scratch, not having to deal with the lookaside might be a noticeable operational improvement — at the cost of needing an updated mirroring tool that knows how to mirror sigstore signatures.

`{skopeo copy, podman push} --sign-by-{sigstore,sigstore-private-key}` for signing; `sigstoreSigned` in `policy.json` for verification; `use-sigstore-attachments` in `registries.d/*.yaml`.)

Yes, we have simple signing working now and that's a requirement from above, but sigstore is the strategic direction and I believe we already have access to x509 keys to use so I can look into signing our content with both mechanisms.

We use `oc image mirror` as our mirroring tool, so long term I see these options:
- Putting in a PR to oc image mirror to make it mirror simple signatures (not strategic)
- Putting in a PR to oc image mirror to make it mirror sigstore tags automatically (maybe, but won't be needed after reference support)
- Adding the sigstore tags to our list of images to mirror in our build processes (unlikely to be picked up by all IBM Cloud Pak teams without huge organizational effort)
- Waiting for references support and putting in a PR to oc image mirror to mirror references too (don't know how long that will take).

After signatures are mirrored I can look at how to help customers set up their policy files.

Short term, I'm thinking of making a quick utility to grab the signatures for mirrored content and using skopeo to verify the manifest signatures, and sha256sum to verify the blob content.

I'll make an update so that "any" only works with a supplied public keys file, and rename the arg.

Also, thank you so much for your help and discussion so far, I'm really at the learning stage of image signing and will be using my new found knowledge to pull out my stirring stick and try and improve our approach across the board :)
@Jamstah thanks for the PR.  You need a rebase and some of the tests are barking.

> @Jamstah thanks for the PR. You need a rebase and some of the tests are barking.

Thanks for the review, done.

Going to assume that ostree-rs-ext is a flake?

> Going to assume that ostree-rs-ext is a flake?

Extremely likely; I have now triggered a re-run.

---

(For other reviewers: Note https://github.com/containers/skopeo/pull/1950#discussion_r1152663358 , please don’t merge yet.)
#1959 updated c/image with the new feature.
I may have slipped in an extra feature.
